intervention_window: Maintain optimal metabolic health through regular exercise (150+ min/week), Mediterranean-style diet, adequate sleep, and stress management. Annual screening is sufficient.
text_chunk: Optimal metabolic health stage with all diabetes-related biomarkers in ideal ranges. Fasting glucose below 85 mg/dL, HbA1c below 5.2%, fasting insulin below 6 uIU/mL, HOMA-IR below 1.0, and triglycerides below 100 mg/dL. This represents the lowest possible diabetes risk with estimated 20+ years from clinical diabetes. Lifestyle maintenance is the primary intervention.

intervention_window: Metabolically normal but not optimal. Lifestyle optimization recommended: weight management, increased physical activity, reduction of refined carbohydrates. Annual metabolic panel recommended.
text_chunk: Metabolically normal stage with glucose and insulin markers within standard reference ranges but above optimal. Fasting glucose 85-95, HbA1c 5.2-5.5%, fasting insulin 6-10, HOMA-IR 1.0-1.5. Approximately 15 years from potential diabetes diagnosis. Early lifestyle optimization at this stage can prevent progression to insulin resistance in most individuals.

intervention_window: Active insulin resistance requiring intensive lifestyle intervention. Weight loss of 5-10%, structured exercise program, dietary modification (low glycemic index). Consider metformin if BMI >35 or multiple risk factors. Biannual monitoring.
text_chunk: Insulin resistant stage with compensatory hyperinsulinemia maintaining normal glucose. HOMA-IR 2.0-3.0 with fasting insulin 10-20 uIU/mL. The Finnish Diabetes Prevention Study and DPP trial demonstrated 58% risk reduction with intensive lifestyle modification at this stage. This is the critical intervention window where progression to pre-diabetes can be prevented.

intervention_window: Pre-diabetes with 5-10% annual conversion rate to diabetes. Intensive lifestyle intervention plus pharmacotherapy consideration (metformin, GLP-1 agonists). Quarterly HbA1c monitoring. TCF7L2 TT carriers have accelerated progression.
text_chunk: Pre-diabetes is defined by fasting glucose 100-125 mg/dL or HbA1c 5.7-6.4%. Annual conversion rate to diabetes is 5-10%. The DPP trial showed 45% risk reduction with metformin and 58% with lifestyle intervention. TCF7L2 rs7903146 TT homozygotes have faster progression. Approximately 5 years from diabetes diagnosis without intervention.

intervention_window: Early diabetes with preserved beta-cell function. Aggressive management with lifestyle modification plus pharmacotherapy (metformin first-line, SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 agonists for cardiovascular benefit). Target HbA1c <7%. Quarterly monitoring.
text_chunk: Early type 2 diabetes with preserved beta-cell function (C-peptide >2.0 ng/mL). UKPDS demonstrated that intensive glycemic control reduces microvascular complications by 25%. SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 agonists provide additional cardiovascular and renal benefits. Early aggressive treatment may preserve beta-cell function longer.

intervention_window: Advanced diabetes with declining beta-cell function and emerging complications. Insulin therapy likely needed. Screen for nephropathy (UACR), retinopathy, neuropathy. Cardiovascular risk management is critical. Multidisciplinary care team recommended.
text_chunk: Advanced diabetes with declining beta-cell function (falling C-peptide) and emerging complications (nephropathy, retinopathy). HbA1c targets may be relaxed to <8% in patients with complications or limited life expectancy. Insulin therapy is often required. SGLT2 inhibitors and finerenone provide renal protection.

intervention_window: Diabetes with established macrovascular and microvascular complications. Focus on complication-specific management, cardiovascular risk reduction, and quality of life. Palliative considerations for advanced renal failure.
text_chunk: Diabetes with established complications: nephropathy (eGFR <30, UACR >300), cardiovascular disease (elevated NT-proBNP, troponin), retinopathy, and neuropathy. Treatment focuses on complication management, cardiovascular protection, and quality of life. Glycemic targets are individualized and often relaxed.

intervention_window: Optimal cardiovascular health. Maintain through Mediterranean diet, regular aerobic and resistance exercise, non-smoking, healthy weight, adequate sleep, and stress management. 10-year ASCVD risk <2.5%.
text_chunk: Optimal cardiovascular profile with all lipid and inflammatory markers in ideal ranges. LDL below 70, HDL above 60, triglycerides below 100, hs-CRP below 0.5, ApoB below 60, and Lp(a) below 30 nmol/L. Represents the lowest cardiovascular risk achievable. Life's Essential 8 score would be near maximum.

intervention_window: Low cardiovascular risk with standard reference range biomarkers. Lifestyle optimization is the primary strategy. Statin therapy not indicated unless other risk factors present. ASCVD risk score typically 2.5-5%.
text_chunk: Low cardiovascular risk stage with lipid and inflammatory markers in standard normal ranges. 10-year ASCVD risk 2.5-5%. No pharmacotherapy indicated in most patients. Lifestyle optimization including regular exercise, healthy diet, and tobacco avoidance remains the primary intervention.

intervention_window: Emerging cardiovascular risk with borderline lipid values and mild inflammation. Lifestyle intensification recommended. Consider statin therapy if additional risk enhancers present (Lp(a) >50, family history, metabolic syndrome). APOE E4 carriers may benefit from earlier statin initiation.
text_chunk: Emerging cardiovascular risk with borderline lipid elevations, mild systemic inflammation (hs-CRP 1.0-2.0), and potential risk enhancers. Elevated Lp(a) above 50 nmol/L is a significant risk enhancer. APOE E4 carriers have higher baseline LDL. The MESA and Framingham studies inform risk stratification at this stage.

intervention_window: Moderate cardiovascular risk. Statin therapy recommended (moderate-intensity). Address metabolic syndrome components: weight loss, triglyceride reduction, blood pressure control. Consider coronary artery calcium (CAC) scoring for further risk stratification.
text_chunk: Moderate cardiovascular risk with LDL 130-160, elevated non-HDL, moderate inflammation, and potential metabolic syndrome. 10-year ASCVD risk 7.5-15%. Moderate-intensity statin therapy recommended. CAC scoring can reclassify risk (CAC 0 may defer statin). PCSK9 genotype may modify statin response.

intervention_window: High cardiovascular risk requiring aggressive lipid management. High-intensity statin therapy indicated (atorvastatin 40-80 mg or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg). Add ezetimibe if LDL remains above 70. Consider PCSK9 inhibitor for very high LDL or statin intolerance (SLCO1B1 genotype).
text_chunk: High cardiovascular risk with markedly elevated atherogenic lipoproteins, systemic inflammation, and additional risk enhancers. 10-year ASCVD risk >15% or equivalent risk. High-intensity statin therapy is the foundation. FOURIER and ODYSSEY trials support PCSK9 inhibitor addition. SLCO1B1 genotype guides statin selection to minimize myopathy risk.

intervention_window: Very high cardiovascular risk, may indicate familial hypercholesterolemia if LDL persistently >190. Maximally tolerated statin plus ezetimibe plus PCSK9 inhibitor. LDL target below 55 mg/dL per ESC guidelines. Screen family members.
text_chunk: Very high cardiovascular risk suggestive of genetic hyperlipidemia (familial hypercholesterolemia if LDL >190), severe systemic inflammation, or subclinical cardiac dysfunction. Combination lipid therapy required: maximum statin + ezetimibe + PCSK9 inhibitor. Target LDL below 55 mg/dL. LDLR/APOB/PCSK9 genetic testing recommended.

intervention_window: Acute cardiovascular event or decompensation. Immediate medical evaluation required. Elevated troponin with rise/fall pattern indicates acute myocardial infarction. Elevated NT-proBNP with symptoms suggests acute heart failure. Emergency department evaluation indicated.
text_chunk: Acute cardiovascular presentation with biomarker evidence of myocardial injury (troponin elevation), cardiac decompensation (NT-proBNP elevation), and/or thrombotic event (D-dimer elevation). Requires immediate medical evaluation and intervention. Post-event, aggressive secondary prevention including high-intensity statin, dual antiplatelet therapy, and comprehensive risk factor management.

intervention_window: Healthy liver function. Maintain through moderate alcohol use (<1 drink/day women, <2 men), healthy weight, and avoidance of hepatotoxins. PNPLA3 GG carriers should be counseled about NAFLD risk even with normal current labs.
text_chunk: Healthy liver with all hepatic biomarkers in optimal ranges. ALT and AST below 25 U/L (updated optimal thresholds), GGT below 30, FIB-4 below 1.0, normal bilirubin and albumin. Even in this stage, PNPLA3 rs738409 GG carriers have elevated baseline NAFLD risk and should maintain aggressive weight management.

intervention_window: Mild hepatic steatosis, likely NAFLD. Weight loss of 3-5% body weight reduces steatosis. Address insulin resistance. PNPLA3 GG carriers may progress faster. Repeat FIB-4 annually. No liver biopsy needed if FIB-4 <1.3.
text_chunk: Mild hepatic steatosis with mildly elevated liver enzymes and insulin resistance markers. FIB-4 below 1.3 effectively excludes significant fibrosis. Weight loss of 3-5% reduces hepatic fat content. PNPLA3 and TM6SF2 genotypes modify progression risk. This stage is reversible with lifestyle modification.

intervention_window: Moderate steatosis with significant enzyme elevation suggesting NASH. Weight loss of 7-10% needed to resolve steatohepatitis. Consider pioglitazone or vitamin E (PIVENS trial). GLP-1 agonists show liver benefit. Refer to hepatology if FIB-4 trends upward.
text_chunk: Moderate hepatic steatosis likely progressing to NASH with higher ALT/AST elevation. Weight loss of 7-10% body weight is needed to resolve steatohepatitis (FLINT and PIVENS trials). PNPLA3 GG carriers have higher risk of NASH at this stage. HSD17B13 TA carriers have attenuated risk. Emerging therapies (resmetirom, semaglutide) show promise.

intervention_window: Early fibrosis (F1-F2) with indeterminate FIB-4. Liver elastography (FibroScan) recommended for further staging. Aggressive lifestyle modification plus pharmacotherapy consideration. Monitor every 6 months. Hepatology referral recommended.
text_chunk: Early hepatic fibrosis (F1-F2) with FIB-4 in indeterminate range (1.3-2.0). Liver elastography recommended: <8 kPa excludes significant fibrosis, >12 kPa suggests advanced fibrosis. This is a critical intervention point where fibrosis may still be reversible with weight loss and targeted therapy. PNPLA3 and TM6SF2 genotypes accelerate fibrosis progression.

intervention_window: Significant fibrosis (F2-F3) approaching advanced stage. Liver biopsy may be indicated for staging. Aggressive pharmacotherapy, hepatology management, and complication screening. Hepatocellular carcinoma surveillance should begin if cirrhosis is suspected.
text_chunk: Significant hepatic fibrosis (F2-F3) with rising AST/ALT ratio and declining platelet count. FIB-4 approaching advanced fibrosis threshold. Liver biopsy or advanced elastography with controlled attenuation parameter (CAP) recommended. HCC surveillance (ultrasound every 6 months) should begin. Treatment options narrowing; clinical trial enrollment should be considered.

intervention_window: Advanced fibrosis/early cirrhosis. Hepatology management mandatory. Screen for varices (EGD), hepatocellular carcinoma (US q6mo), hepatic decompensation. Consider liver transplant evaluation if MELD score rising.
text_chunk: Advanced fibrosis to early cirrhosis with FIB-4 above 2.67, declining albumin and platelet count, and prolonged INR. MELD score should be calculated for transplant prioritization. HCC surveillance mandatory. Variceal screening indicated. Compensated cirrhosis can remain stable for years with appropriate management, but decompensation risk is significant.

intervention_window: Established cirrhosis with synthetic dysfunction. Transplant evaluation if eligible. Manage complications: ascites, varices, hepatic encephalopathy, HCC. Child-Pugh scoring for prognosis.
text_chunk: Established cirrhosis with hepatic synthetic dysfunction (low albumin, elevated INR, hyperbilirubinemia) and portal hypertension (thrombocytopenia). Child-Pugh and MELD scores guide management and transplant listing. Five-year survival depends on decompensation status. Genetic modifiers (PNPLA3, HSD17B13) affect progression rate even at this stage.

intervention_window: Optimal thyroid function. Maintain through adequate iodine and selenium intake. Monitor annually if family history of thyroid disease or if TPO antibodies are present.
text_chunk: Optimal thyroid function with TSH in the ideal range of 0.5-2.0 mIU/L, normal free T4 and T3, negative TPO antibodies, and normal reverse T3. This represents healthy thyroid-pituitary axis function with minimal disease risk. DIO2 rs225014 genotype should be documented for future reference if thyroid treatment ever becomes necessary.

intervention_window: Subclinical hypothyroidism with elevated TSH and normal free T4. If TPO antibodies positive, 4% annual progression to overt hypothyroidism. Consider levothyroxine if TSH >7, symptoms present, or planning pregnancy. Repeat TSH in 6-12 weeks to confirm.
text_chunk: Subclinical hypothyroidism defined by TSH 4.0-10.0 with normal free T4. Present in 5-10% of adults, more common in women and elderly. TPO antibody positivity predicts 4% annual progression to overt hypothyroidism. Treatment is recommended if TSH >7, pregnancy planned, or cardiovascular risk factors present. The Colorado Thyroid Disease Prevalence Study established these screening guidelines.

intervention_window: Mild overt hypothyroidism with low free T4. Levothyroxine initiation recommended. Start at 1.6 mcg/kg/day for young healthy adults, lower dose (25-50 mcg) for elderly or cardiac patients. DIO2 genotype may guide T4/T3 combination therapy consideration.
text_chunk: Mild overt hypothyroidism with TSH 10-20 and low free T4. Levothyroxine replacement is standard treatment. DIO2 rs225014 AA homozygotes may benefit from combination T4/T3 therapy. Dose titration targets TSH 0.5-2.5 mIU/L. Recheck TSH 6-8 weeks after dose changes. Most patients achieve euthyroidism with levothyroxine monotherapy.

intervention_window: Moderate hypothyroidism with secondary hyperlipidemia. Start levothyroxine with careful dose titration. Cholesterol should be rechecked after achieving euthyroidism before initiating statin therapy, as thyroid-related hyperlipidemia is reversible.
text_chunk: Moderate hypothyroidism causing secondary metabolic effects including hyperlipidemia and potential edema. Elevated total cholesterol and LDL are common and often normalize with thyroid hormone replacement. Statin initiation should be deferred until euthyroidism is achieved and lipids rechecked. Cardiac monitoring recommended during levothyroxine initiation in elderly patients.

intervention_window: Severe overt hypothyroidism requiring prompt treatment. May present with myxedema. Full replacement dose levothyroxine needed. Monitor for cardiac effects during repletion. Elevated CK is common and resolves with treatment.
text_chunk: Severe overt hypothyroidism with markedly elevated TSH and very low thyroid hormones. May cause myxedema, pericardial effusion, elevated CK, and cognitive impairment. Requires prompt levothyroxine replacement. In elderly or cardiac patients, start low and increase gradually to avoid cardiac complications. Myxedema coma is a medical emergency requiring IV levothyroxine.

intervention_window: Hashimoto thyroiditis confirmed by high TPO antibodies. Thyroid function may fluctuate (hashitoxicosis followed by hypothyroidism). Selenium supplementation (200 mcg/day) may reduce TPO antibodies. Lifetime monitoring required. Screen for other autoimmune conditions.
text_chunk: Hashimoto thyroiditis is the most common autoimmune thyroid disease, characterized by very high TPO antibodies (>500 IU/mL). Thyroid function may fluctuate before settling into permanent hypothyroidism. Associated with HLA-DR3, CTLA4, and PTPN22 variants. Selenium supplementation may reduce antibody titers. Screen for associated autoimmune conditions (celiac disease, pernicious anemia, type 1 diabetes).

intervention_window: Optimal iron status. Maintain through balanced diet with adequate heme and non-heme iron sources. HFE genotyping recommended if family history of hemochromatosis. No supplementation needed.
text_chunk: Optimal iron status with ferritin 40-150 ng/mL, TSAT 25-45%, and normal hemoglobin. This range avoids both deficiency and overload risks. HFE C282Y carriers should have baseline TSAT and ferritin documented for future monitoring comparison. Annual monitoring sufficient in the absence of risk factors.

intervention_window: Borderline iron depletion with preserved hemoglobin. Oral iron supplementation recommended (ferrous sulfate 325 mg every other day for optimal absorption). Evaluate for occult blood loss in men and postmenopausal women.
text_chunk: Borderline iron depletion with ferritin 15-30 ng/mL indicating depleted stores before anemia develops. TSAT approaching the 20% threshold. Iron supplementation at this stage prevents progression to frank anemia. Every-other-day dosing improves absorption by 40% compared to daily dosing. Evaluate for blood loss (GI, menstrual) if unexplained.

intervention_window: Iron deficiency anemia. Identify and treat underlying cause (menorrhagia, GI blood loss, celiac disease). Oral iron for mild-moderate; IV iron for severe, intolerant, or malabsorptive patients. Recheck ferritin at 3 months.
text_chunk: Iron deficiency anemia with low ferritin (<15), low TSAT (<15%), elevated TIBC, microcytic anemia (MCV <80). Most common nutritional deficiency worldwide. Must identify cause: menorrhagia, GI blood loss (colonoscopy/EGD in men and postmenopausal women), celiac disease, or dietary insufficiency. IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) for rapid repletion when oral iron is insufficient.

intervention_window: Mild iron overload. HFE genotyping indicated. If C282Y homozygous, begin therapeutic phlebotomy (target ferritin <50). If HFE negative, evaluate secondary causes (transfusions, ineffective erythropoiesis, metabolic syndrome).
text_chunk: Mild iron overload with ferritin 300-500 and TSAT above 45%. HFE genotyping is mandatory at this stage. C282Y homozygotes should begin therapeutic phlebotomy early to prevent organ damage. If HFE negative, consider secondary causes: chronic transfusions, myelodysplasia, NAFLD (dysmetabolic iron overload). This stage is fully reversible with phlebotomy.

intervention_window: Moderate iron overload with early organ effects. Urgent phlebotomy program needed. Screen for hepatic fibrosis (FIB-4, elastography), diabetes, cardiomyopathy, and hypogonadism. Target ferritin below 50 ng/mL with weekly phlebotomy.
text_chunk: Moderate iron overload with ferritin 500-1000 and organ involvement beginning (elevated ALT, rising HbA1c from pancreatic iron deposition, declining testosterone from pituitary siderosis). Weekly therapeutic phlebotomy removes approximately 250 mg iron per unit. Liver MRI (T2*) can quantify hepatic iron concentration. Cardiac MRI T2* assessment if ferritin >1000.

intervention_window: Clinical hemochromatosis with organ damage. Intensive phlebotomy program. Screen family members. Liver biopsy or MRI for fibrosis staging. Manage end-organ damage: diabetes, cardiomyopathy, arthropathy, hypogonadism. Some organ damage (arthropathy, diabetes) may not fully reverse.
text_chunk: Clinical hemochromatosis with ferritin >1000, TSAT >80%, and established organ damage. Bronze diabetes (pancreatic iron), hepatic cirrhosis (10-33% of untreated C282Y homozygotes), cardiomyopathy, and arthropathy. Intensive phlebotomy is urgent. HCC surveillance required if cirrhosis present. First-degree relatives require HFE testing. Early detection in relatives prevents this stage entirely.

intervention_window: Optimal nutritional status across key micronutrients. Maintain through varied whole-food diet with adequate fruits, vegetables, omega-3-rich fish, and sun exposure. Genotype-specific supplementation if MTHFR, BCMO1, VDR, or FADS1 variants present.
text_chunk: Optimal nutritional biomarker profile with vitamin D 40-60 ng/mL, B12 above 500 pg/mL, folate above 10 ng/mL, omega-3 index above 8%, and adequate mineral status. This profile is associated with lowest risk of nutritional-related chronic disease. MTHFR, VDR, GC, FADS1, and BCMO1 genotypes should guide ongoing supplementation strategy.

intervention_window: Suboptimal but not deficient nutritional status. Targeted supplementation recommended based on genotype: methylfolate for MTHFR TT, preformed vitamin A for BCMO1 TT, higher omega-3 for FADS1 TT. Dietary counseling.
text_chunk: Suboptimal nutritional status with levels above deficiency thresholds but below optimal. Vitamin D insufficiency (20-30 ng/mL) affects bone health and immunity. Functional B12 deficiency may exist despite normal serum levels (check MMA and homocysteine). Omega-3 index 4-8% indicates moderate cardiovascular risk. Genotype-guided supplementation is most effective at this stage.

intervention_window: Active nutritional deficiency requiring supplementation. Vitamin D3 50,000 IU weekly x 8 weeks then maintenance. B12 injections if pernicious anemia or malabsorption. Methylfolate for MTHFR variants. High-dose omega-3 supplementation.
text_chunk: Active nutritional deficiency with clinical risk. Vitamin D below 20 ng/mL increases fracture, fall, autoimmune, and cardiovascular risk. B12 below 200 with elevated MMA confirms true deficiency requiring repletion. Folate below 3 causes megaloblastic anemia. Omega-3 index below 4% indicates high cardiovascular risk. Aggressive repletion followed by maintenance supplementation.

intervention_window: Severe nutritional deficiency with clinical manifestations. B12 <100 with macrocytosis and neuropathy requires urgent B12 injections (1000 mcg IM daily x 7 days). Severe vitamin D deficiency requires high-dose repletion. Evaluate for underlying malabsorption.
text_chunk: Severe nutritional deficiency with clinical manifestations: severe vitamin D deficiency (<10) with muscle weakness and bone pain, B12 <100 with megaloblastic anemia and subacute combined degeneration, severe folate deficiency with pancytopenia. Requires urgent parenteral repletion. Evaluate for pernicious anemia (intrinsic factor antibodies), celiac disease, and inflammatory bowel disease.

intervention_window: Genetically-driven nutritional deficiency requiring personalized supplementation. MTHFR TT: methylfolate 1-5 mg/day plus methylcobalamin. VDR variants: higher vitamin D doses. FADS1 TT: 2-4g EPA/DHA daily. BCMO1 TT: preformed retinol instead of beta-carotene.
text_chunk: Genetically-driven nutritional deficiency where standard supplementation is inadequate. MTHFR C677T TT homozygotes require methylfolate (not folic acid) and methylcobalamin. VDR and GC variants may require 2-3x standard vitamin D doses. FADS1 variants need higher omega-3 supplementation. BCMO1 variants need preformed vitamin A. Genotype-guided nutrition is the cornerstone of precision supplementation.

intervention_window: Impaired methylation with elevated homocysteine despite B12 and folate in normal range. Requires methylated B-vitamins: methylfolate, methylcobalamin, and P5P (active B6). Trim ethylglycine (betaine) may provide additional methyl donor support. Monitor homocysteine response.
text_chunk: Impaired methylation cycle with elevated homocysteine suggesting inadequate one-carbon metabolism. MTHFR variants are the most common cause. Functional deficiency of B12, folate, and B6 can occur despite normal serum levels. Treatment requires methylated forms: methylfolate, methylcobalamin, and pyridoxal-5-phosphate. Betaine (trimethylglycine) provides an alternative methylation pathway. SAM/SAH ratio is the gold standard for methylation status.
